text.title: vui-testing-best-practices Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for vui-testing-best-practices as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: LLM judge sees the expected answer when scoring naturalness — anchoring bias inflates scores.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Score naturalness blind, then compute correctness separately. Judges with access to expected answers rate responses as more natural when they are also correct, masking naturalness failures in correct responses.

failure-mode.description: Using absolute 1-5 naturalness scores instead of pairwise comparison.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Absolute LLM scores are unreliable across sessions. Pairwise comparison ("Is A or B more natural?") produces more consistent results and surfaces meaningful differences between prompt variants.

failure-mode.description: Using the same TTS vendor for test fixtures as for product output — produces false-high naturalness scores.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Same-vendor TTS creates circular evaluation: the LLM judge finds familiar prosody patterns more natural. Use a different TTS engine for fixture generation than for product rendering.

failure-mode.description: Sending raw user-recorded audio to a cloud LLM judge.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Raw audio may contain PII, bystander voices, and background conversations. Always transcribe locally first, redact PII, then judge the text. Never pass raw audio to an external model.

failure-mode.description: Running the full LLM-judge suite concurrently without concurrency caps, hitting 429 rate limits that cause silent test passes.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: 429 responses can be swallowed as empty results rather than errors. Cap concurrency at provider rate limits and treat any 429 as a test failure, not a skip.
